name: HMS H 28 (N 28)
events: The history of HMS H 28 as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine as well as several other files.
This page was last updated in November 2017.

HMS H 28 was the only submarine to have carried out offensive war Patrols in both World War 1 and World War 2. Two Italian submarines and two American submarines also served in both World War 1 and World War 2, but did not carry out offensive patrols in both wars. (1)

2 Oct 1939 HMS H 28 (Lt. D.E. Mansfield, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Plymouth with HMS Volunteer (Lt.Cdr. H. Gartside-Tippinge, RN). Later the same day she departed Plymouth for Portland. She made that passage together with HMS Trident. (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN). The submarines were escorted by HMS Shikari (Cdr. J.H. Lewis, RN). (2)

10 May 1940 HMS H 28 (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS H 44 (Lt. E.D. Norman, RN), HMS H 49 (Lt. M.A. Langley, RN) and HMS H 50 (Lt. A.R. Cheyne, RN) shifted from Portland to Portsmouth. They were escorted by HMS Shemara (Cdr.(Retd. H. Buckle, RN).
Later the same day all four submarines departed Portsmouth again for patrol in the North Sea. They were escorted until 1700/11 by HMS Foxglove (Lt.Cdr. T.I.S. Bell, RN). (6)

18 May 1940 At 1400 hours, HMS H 28 (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) joined HMS H 44 (Lt. E.D. Norman, RN) and their escort HMS Warrior II (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) off South Foreland. The two submarines and their escort then proceeded to Portsmouth where they arrived at 0530/19. (6)

29 May 1940 HMS H 28 (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) departed Portland for her 2nd war patrol. She is ordered to patrol in the English Channel.

She departed Portland together with HMS H 44 (Lt. E.D. Norman, RN), HMS H 49 (Lt. M.A. Langley, RN) and HMS H 50 (Lt. A.R. Cheyne, RN). They made rendez vous with HMS Warrior II (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) at 2030/29 which escorted them until 0345/30 when the submarines proceeded to their patrol areas.
[No log is available for this period and the patrol report does not give positions, therefore no map can be displayed.] (7)

11 Oct 1940 HMS H 28 (Lt. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN) attacked an enemy transport in a convoy off Texel in position 53°11'N, 04°40'E. Two torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.

The convoy attacked was made up of the German Meise (821 GRT, built 1918) and Dutch (in German service) Nijenburgh (399 GRT, built 1939). They were escorted by the auxiliary submarine chasers UJ-A, UJ-C and UJ-F. UJ C reported being missed astern by one torpedo by a margin of about 10 meters.
1700 hours - Sighted an enemy convoy. Commenced attack.

1705 hours - Fired two torpedoes against an enemy transport from 3000 yards. No hits were obtained. It is thought the torpedoes missed astern due to the speed being underestimated. A hunt for the submarine was now started in which three depth charges were dropped but these did no damage. (7)
18 Oct 1940 HMS H 28 (Lt. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN) ended her 7th war patrol at Harwich.
Earlier that day, at 0805 hours, H 28 was machine gunned by a German Heinkel 111 aircraft in position 52°12'N, 02°04'E. H 28 sustained no damage. (7)

16 Dec 1941 HMS H 28 (Lt. J.C. Ogle, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay for her 8th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay as it is feared the German battlecruisers Scharnhorst and Gneisenau and heavy cruiser Prinz Eugen might breack out from Brest.
Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS Otway (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN). The submarines were escorted by HrMs Z 5 (Lt. J.J. Steensma, RNN) until 1130/18. (7)
16 Dec 1941 For the daily positions of HMS H 28 during this patrol and the passage back from Falmouth to Rothesay see the map below.
21 Dec 1941 HMS H 28 (Lt. J.C. Ogle, DSC, RN) left her patrol area for Falmouth. (7)

24 Dec 1941 HMS H 28 (Lt. J.C. Ogle, DSC, RN) arrived at Falmouth. She had made rendez-vous around 0920 hours with HMS H 50 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, RN) and HMS Otway (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN). THey were escorted in by the torpedo boat HrMs Z 5 (Lt. J.J. Steensma, RNN) and MS trawler HMS Fluellen (T/Lt. D.P. Richardson, RNVR).

After fuelling, provisioning and having effected some repairs H 28 departed Falmouth at 2100 to resume her patrol. She was escorted out until 2320 hours by HMS Night Hawk (T/Lt. B.J.P. Kelly, RNVR). (7)
30 Dec 1941 HMS H 28 (Lt. J.C. Ogle, DSC, RN) left her patrol area for Falmouth. (7)
1 Jan 1942 HMS H 28 (Lt. J.C. Ogle, DSC, RN) ended her 8th war patrol at Falmouth.
She made rendez-vous with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) off Lizard Rock at 1022 hours. Both ships arrived at Falmouth shortly before 1700 hours. (7)
